policy_statement: Analyze opportunities to meet diversity and workforce goals in clean energy resource procurement (ES.4-4).
mechanism_description: Conduct analysis to identify ways to integrate diversity and workforce goals into clean energy resource procurement, strengthening clean energy workforce capacity and delivery.
primary_causal_pathway: Workforce/procurement governance → ↑ clean energy project delivery capacity → ↑ clean energy deployment → ↓ fossil generation → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in human activity/system: The city performs procurement-focused analysis and embeds diversity/workforce goals into clean energy procurement processes, shaping contractor set, bid evaluation, and workforce development expectations. (2) Mediating process: Improved labor supply, contractor participation, and project execution capability reduces bottlenecks and increases the throughput/reliability of clean energy resource procurement and delivery. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Increased clean energy deployment displaces fossil-based electricity/energy supply, lowering lifecycle CO₂e emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is enabling greater/faster clean energy procurement and deployment, thereby displacing fossil fuel energy and reducing GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: governance/workforce-capacity actions enable increased clean energy procurement/deployment, which reduces fossil fuel use and emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: It is not chiefly about using less energy for the same service (resource efficiency), reducing hazard exposure (adaptation), or enhancing ecosystems (nature-based solutions). The most material climate outcome is emissions reduction via clean energy scale-up.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy instance is purely an equity/workforce reporting exercise with no linkage to procurement outcomes, climate impact could be negligible; however, within this canonical mechanism the intended effect is capacity-building for clean energy delivery, which maps most directly to mitigation when it succeeds.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: All policies sharing this mechanism (integrating diversity/workforce goals into clean energy procurement via analysis) are designed to strengthen delivery capacity for clean energy resources; the only plausible climate-relevant impact is enabling more clean energy and thus lower emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any finer-grained subtype (e.g., enabling policy, just transition/equity co-benefit, procurement reform vs training) requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level classification.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Indirectness is the main edge case: analysis/procurement workforce goals may not guarantee additional clean energy deployment, so realized mitigation impact can vary widely. If implemented only as workforce diversification without affecting procurement volume or project delivery, climate effects may be minimal (but the dominant intended pathway remains mitigation enablement).
key_indicators: "Engage in analysis"; "identify opportunities"; "meet diversity and workforce goals"; "procurement of clean energy resources"
co_benefits: Workforce development and job access; equity/diversity in contracting; local economic development
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be coded as a procurement-requirement if the text specified embedding requirements (e.g., RFP scoring, contractor targets); here it is limited to analysis, so planning-strategy/enabling is the best fit.
